concepts.name: Scalars and Vectors
concepts.description: Understand what physical quantities have only magnitude (scalars) vs magnitude + direction (vectors).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Visualize difference between scalar vs vector using arrows/values

learning_objectives: Distinguish between scalar and vector quantities.
Explain why direction matters for vector quantities.

concepts.name: One-Dimensional Motion: position, speed, velocity
concepts.description: Describe position, speed and velocity along a line; interpret simple motion patterns qualitatively.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to move an object along a line and observe position/time, velocity/time visually

cross_domain_links.notes: Motion can be represented as a time-based function; graphs help visualization

learning_objectives: Define and distinguish position, speed, and velocity.
Visualize motion along a line over time.
Interpret basic motion qualitatively.

concepts.name: Acceleration & Velocity Change
concepts.description: Conceptualize acceleration: how velocity changes over time in 1-D motion contexts.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate an object accelerating or decelerating along 1-D path; observe velocity/time changes

learning_objectives: Explain what acceleration means (change in velocity over time).
Relate acceleration to real-world motion changes (speeding up, slowing down).

concepts.name: Force (qualitative)
concepts.description: Basic notion of a force: pushes/pulls that cause motion change — intuitive understanding without heavy math.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Visualize applying a force to an object and observe motion change qualitatively

learning_objectives: Understand what a force is (a push or pull).
Explain how applying a force can change an object’s motion.

concepts.name: Basic Energy: Kinetic and Potential (qualitative)
concepts.description: Introductory idea of energy, different forms — kinetic and potential energy qualitatively in simple contexts.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Demonstrate energy as a concept; show transformation between kinetic and potential energy visually (e.g. object thrown up/down).

learning_objectives: Differentiate between kinetic and potential energy.
Visualize energy transformation in simple motion contexts.
Build intuition for energy conservation in simple scenarios.

concepts.name: Gravity & Weight (qualitative)
concepts.description: Basic idea of gravity, weight, and why objects fall or feel heavy — intuitive understanding.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate objects under gravity: free-fall, weight, falling vs slow drop, observe motion change.

learning_objectives: Explain what gravity is and how it affects objects.
Visualize free-fall motion and weight under gravity.
Relate mass, gravity, and weight in intuitive scenarios.

concepts.name: Basic Waves & Sound (qualitative)
concepts.description: Intro to wave phenomena: disturbance propagation, waves and sound qualitatively — understand wave as propagation of energy/disturbance.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Visualize wave propagation, e.g. wave on string, simple sound wave, without heavy math.

learning_objectives: Understand what a wave is (a propagating disturbance).
Relate basic wave features (crest/trough, propagation) to phenomena like sound or water waves.

concepts.name: Basic Light, Reflection & Shadows (qualitative)
concepts.description: Observation-based understanding of light behavior: rays, shadows, reflection qualitatively in simple contexts.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate light rays, reflection, shadows, simple ray diagrams visually.

learning_objectives: Visualize basic light behavior (rays).
Explain how shadows and reflections form.

concepts.name: Intro to Measurement & Units (length, time, mass)
concepts.description: Understanding units of measurement that physics uses (meter, second, kilogram), basic measurement concepts and unit conversion — foundation for quantification.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to explore measurement units, convert between units, understand baseline measurements.

cross_domain_links.notes: Basic arithmetic and number sense needed for measurement and conversions

learning_objectives: Recognize key physical units: meters, seconds, kilograms.
Perform simple unit conversions correctly.
Understand the importance of consistent units in physics.

concepts.name: 2D Motion: Velocity & Acceleration Vectors, Trajectories
concepts.description: Extend 1-D motion to 2-D: understand motion in a plane, decompose vectors, analyze trajectories under constant acceleration (e.g. projectile motion).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to set initial velocity vector, angle and magnitude, and simulate projectile motion; visualize position, velocity, acceleration over time

cross_domain_links.notes: Vector operations and coordinate geometry for decomposition

learning_objectives: Represent motion in 2D using vector decomposition.
Predict and visualize the trajectory of a projectile under gravity.
Relate velocity and acceleration vectors to real motion paths.

concepts.name: Newton’s Laws of Motion (quantitative)
concepts.description: Formal study of Newton's three laws: how forces lead to acceleration, concept of inertia and action-reaction; apply to various scenarios quantitatively (forces, mass, acceleration, friction).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Let user apply forces (single or multiple), adjust mass, friction; simulate resulting motion and analyze forces, acceleration, velocity, displacement.

cross_domain_links.notes: Continuous change often expressed with calculus for acceleration, velocity relationships

learning_objectives: Apply Newton's laws to compute motion given forces and mass.
Use free-body diagrams to represent forces and predict motion.
Understand and quantify inertia, net force, acceleration relationships.

concepts.name: Work, Energy & Power (quantitative)
concepts.description: Define and calculate work, kinetic energy, potential energy (gravitational), and power; explore energy transformations and work–energy theorem in mechanical systems.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate mechanical systems (object lifting, falling, sliding) to observe work done, energy conversions and compute energies and power over time

learning_objectives: Compute work done by forces in various contexts.
Compute kinetic and potential energy and understand energy conservation principles.
Use energy and power calculations to analyze motion and work scenarios.

concepts.name: Momentum & Collisions (elastic and inelastic)
concepts.description: Understand momentum, impulse; analyze collisions in 1D and 2D — elastic vs inelastic collisions, conservation laws for momentum and kinetic energy (when applicable).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate collisions between objects with varying masses/velocities; observe post-collision velocities, check conservation laws; allow change of elasticity parameter.

learning_objectives: Define momentum and impulse.
Apply conservation of momentum to collisions.
Distinguish between elastic and inelastic collisions, and compute outcomes.

concepts.name: Circular Motion & Centripetal Force
concepts.description: Introduce motion along circular paths: centripetal acceleration & force, uniform circular motion, period and frequency relations; analyze motion on a circular track qualitatively and quantitatively.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate object moving in circle given speed/radius; visualize centripetal force vector, acceleration vector; vary speed/radius to observe effects.

learning_objectives: Explain centripetal acceleration and force in circular motion.
Quantitatively relate speed, radius, and period/frequency for circular motion.
Visualize forces and velocities in circular motion contexts.

concepts.name: Simple Harmonic Motion (springs, pendulums)
concepts.description: Study oscillatory motion: springs, mass-spring systems, pendulums; analyze period, amplitude, restoring force qualitatively & quantitatively (small-angle approx, Hooke’s law).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate oscillatory systems (spring-mass, pendulum), allow adjustment of parameters (mass, spring constant, length) to observe oscillation behavior, period, energy conversions.

learning_objectives: Describe simple harmonic motion and restoring force.
Compute period/frequency for spring and pendulum systems (approx).
Visualize oscillations and energy exchanges in oscillatory motion.

concepts.name: Wave Fundamentals: wavelength, frequency, wave speed
concepts.description: Formalize wave behavior: define wavelength, frequency, amplitude, wave speed; understand mechanical waves in media; relation between wave properties and medium characteristics.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate wave propagation on a string or medium, allow adjusting frequency/wavelength/medium characteristics, visualize wave propagation, measure speed, amplitude, period.

learning_objectives: Define wavelength, frequency, amplitude, and wave speed for mechanical waves.
Relate wave properties and medium characteristics to wave behavior.
Visualize wave propagation and measure wave parameters.

concepts.name: Basic Electrostatics: Charge, Coulomb’s Law (intro)
concepts.description: Introduce electric charge, Coulomb’s law for point charges, concept of electric force/field at qualitative and simplified quantitative level.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate two or more point charges, allow users to change magnitude and sign, visualize forces/fields and compute force magnitude/direction.

learning_objectives: Understand electric charge and Coulomb’s law.
Compute electrostatic force between charges.
Visualize electric forces and fields for point-charge configurations.

concepts.name: Conservation Laws & Energy/Momentum Conservation in Collisions
concepts.description: Explore conservation of momentum and energy (when applicable), understand conditions for conservation (isolated systems), analyze collisions, and understand system constraints and energy transformations.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate collisions or closed-system interactions; track total energy, kinetic energy, momentum before and after to examine conservation laws under different conditions (elastic, inelastic, external forces).

learning_objectives: Apply conservation of momentum in collision scenarios.
Determine whether kinetic energy is conserved and under what conditions.
Analyze energy transformations and system boundaries.

concepts.name: Rotational Dynamics & Rigid Body Motion
concepts.description: Study rotation of rigid bodies: torque, moment of inertia, angular momentum, rotational kinetic energy, and their relation to translational motion.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate rotating rigid bodies under various torques; visualize angular velocity, inertia, energy and momentum; explore stability and rotational motion.

learning_objectives: Compute torque, moment of inertia, angular momentum for rigid bodies.
Analyze rotational motion and its relation to force and energy.
Understand conservation of angular momentum in isolated systems.

concepts.name: Fluid Mechanics: Pressure, Buoyancy, Flow & Continuity
concepts.description: Understand fluid statics and dynamics: pressure in fluids, buoyancy, fluid flow, continuity equation, basic fluid behavior qualitatively and quantitatively.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate fluid containers, buoyancy, flowing fluids; visualize pressure, density, flow lines; experiment with fluid parameters.

learning_objectives: Compute fluid pressure and buoyant force in static fluids.
Understand and apply the continuity equation for incompressible flow.
Visualize fluid flow behavior and effects of density/pressure changes.

concepts.name: Wave Optics & Electromagnetic Waves
concepts.description: Study detailed wave phenomena: interference, diffraction, polarization, electromagnetic wave propagation, wave–matter interaction, basic optics theory, wave–particle aspects intro.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate wave interference, diffraction, polarization, EM wave propagation; visualize fields and resulting patterns; allow parameter variation (wavelength, slit width, medium).

learning_objectives: Describe interference, diffraction and polarization of waves.
Simulate electromagnetic wave propagation and interactions.
Understand relationship between wave properties (wavelength, frequency) and observed phenomena in optics.

concepts.name: Thermodynamics & Statistical Physics (intro)
concepts.description: Formal study of thermodynamics: laws of thermodynamics, heat, work, internal energy, entropy (qualitative + introductory quantitative); basic statistical ideas for systems of many particles.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate thermodynamic processes (heating, cooling, compression, expansion), calculate work, heat, temperature changes; optionally simulate ideal gas behavior; allow parameter variation.

learning_objectives: Understand and apply the laws of thermodynamics (energy conservation, entropy).
Model simple thermodynamic processes and compute heat, work, temperature changes.
Introduce statistical thinking for many-particle systems.

concepts.name: Electricity & Magnetism (fields, circuits, Maxwell basics)
concepts.description: Comprehensive study of electric and magnetic fields, field lines, potential, circuits, electromagnetic waves and introduction to fundamentals of Maxwell’s equations (qualitative/intro-calculus level).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate charge distributions, fields, circuits, field propagation; visualize field lines, potentials, charge interactions; allow parameter variation in circuits and fields.

learning_objectives: Compute and visualize electric and magnetic fields for various charge/current configurations.
Analyze circuits, field interactions and EM wave propagation.
Understand the concept of field and potential, and basic Maxwell's equations implications.

concepts.name: Special Relativity (intro)
concepts.description: Introduction to the theory of special relativity: relativity of simultaneity, time dilation, length contraction, mass–energy equivalence, simple relativistic dynamics.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Visualize relativistic effects (time dilation, length contraction), inertial frames transformation, velocity addition; allow parameter variation of speed to observe relativistic phenomena.

learning_objectives: Explain core concepts of special relativity (time dilation, length contraction, simultaneity).
Apply Lorentz transformations to simple scenarios.
Understand mass–energy equivalence and limitations of classical mechanics at high speeds.

concepts.name: Lagrangian & Hamiltonian Mechanics (Analytical Mechanics)
concepts.description: Formulation of mechanics using variational principles: Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formalisms, generalized coordinates, canonical momenta, Poisson brackets, symmetries & conservation via Noether’s theorem.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow definition of a system via Lagrangian/Hamiltonian; simulate motion, derive equations of motion, show phase-space trajectories, explore conserved quantities under symmetries.

cross_domain_links.notes: Solving Euler–Lagrange or Hamilton's equations relies on differential equations

learning_objectives: Derive equations of motion from a given Lagrangian/Hamiltonian.
Understand generalized coordinates and canonical momenta.
Use symmetry principles to deduce conserved quantities via Noether’s theorem.
Analyze phase-space trajectories and stability.

concepts.name: Quantum Mechanics (Non-relativistic, Many-Body & Perturbation Theory)
concepts.description: Foundations of quantum mechanics: Hilbert spaces, operators, wavefunctions, time-independent & time-dependent Schrödinger equation; perturbation theory, multi-particle systems, identical particles, spin and angular momentum, basic many-body theory.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to define simple quantum systems (1D/3D wells, harmonic oscillator, hydrogen atom), compute eigenstates / eigenvalues, visualize wavefunctions, experiment with perturbations, combine multiple particles to observe many-body behavior.

cross_domain_links.notes: Quantum states and operators require vector spaces, eigenvalues, inner products

learning_objectives: Solve time-independent and time-dependent Schrödinger equation for basic potentials.
Understand the role of operators, eigenstates, and observable measurements in quantum mechanics.
Apply perturbation theory for small corrections.
Model simple many-body quantum systems, understand indistinguishability, symmetrization/antisymmetrization.

concepts.name: Quantum Field Theory & Relativistic Quantum Mechanics (intro)
concepts.description: Introduction to quantum field theory (QFT) and relativistic quantum mechanics: field quantization, Dirac equation, particle creation/annihilation, basics of gauge fields, simple interactions; conceptual understanding bridging quantum mechanics and special relativity.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to define simple field theories, visualize field propagation, particle creation/annihilation, draw Feynman diagrams, explore basic interactions (e.g. in QED), emphasize conceptual understanding.

cross_domain_links.notes: Field Lagrangians build on classical analytical mechanics
Field theory uses advanced mathematics like tensors and differential geometry

learning_objectives: Understand the necessity of field quantization when combining quantum mechanics and relativity.
Formulate simple Lagrangian field theories and derive their equations of motion.
Interpret basic field interactions using simple diagrams and conceptual tools.
Recognize the limits of nonrelativistic quantum mechanics and where QFT becomes relevant.

concepts.name: Statistical Mechanics & Advanced Thermodynamics
concepts.description: Statistical physics: micro-state ensembles, canonical & grand canonical ensembles, partition functions, connection to thermodynamics, emergent macroscopic properties from microscopic states, phase transitions, fluctuations, statistical description of many-particle systems.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Simulate large particle ensembles (e.g. ideal gas, spin systems), compute statistical properties, simulate thermodynamic processes, observe emergent behavior (temperature, entropy, fluctuations, phase transitions).

cross_domain_links.notes: Statistical mechanics underlies both physics and chemical thermodynamics

learning_objectives: Derive macroscopic thermodynamic properties from microscopic statistical ensembles.
Compute partition functions and relate to energy, entropy, free energy.
Model phase transitions and fluctuations in many-particle systems.
Understand connections between statistical mechanics, thermodynamics, and thermodynamic laws.

concepts.name: Computational Physics & Numerical Methods
concepts.description: Use numerical methods to simulate physical systems: solve ordinary/partial differential equations (ODE/PDE), implement simulations for mechanics, fluids, statistical systems; Monte Carlo methods; computational approximation techniques for complex systems.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Provide a coding sandbox for users to implement numerical solvers (ODE / PDE / Monte-Carlo) for physical systems (e.g. pendulum, wave equation, diffusion, N-body), visualize results, and analyze behavior.

cross_domain_links.notes: Computational physics relies on programming, memory management, efficient simulation

learning_objectives: Implement numerical solvers for ODEs & PDEs governing physical systems.
Simulate complex physical phenomena not solvable analytically.
Analyze simulation results, validate against theory, handle numerical stability.

concepts.name: Relativistic & Gravitational Physics (intro to General Relativity)
concepts.description: Study of relativistic dynamics beyond special relativity; introduction to curved spacetime, basic general relativity concepts, spacetime metric, geodesics, gravity as geometry (qualitative/intro mathematical).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Visualize curved spacetime around masses, geodesic orbits, gravitational time dilation, simple black-hole metrics; provide conceptual tools without full tensor mathematics if desired.

cross_domain_links.notes: General relativity requires differential geometry and tensor calculus

learning_objectives: Understand concept of spacetime as a curved manifold representing gravity.
Visualize motion (geodesics) in curved spacetime around massive bodies.
Appreciate difference between Newtonian gravity and general relativistic gravity.

concepts.name: Condensed Matter & Many-Body Physics (intro)
concepts.description: Study of many-particle systems in solid state, statistical emergent phenomena, basic models (e.g. Fermi gas, lattice models), introduction to many-body approaches: mean-field theory, second quantization, basic solid-state phenomena (crystals, band theory, phonons).

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow users to model simplified many-body systems (e.g. electron gas, lattice), compute band structure, density of states, simulate basic thermal and quantum effects in solids, visualize lattice and energy bands.

cross_domain_links.notes: Atomic/molecular quantum models underpin condensed matter behavior

learning_objectives: Model many-particle quantum systems in simplified contexts.
Compute and interpret band structures, density of states, collective phenomena.
Relate microscopic interactions to macroscopic solid-state properties.

concepts.name: Non-linear Dynamics, Chaos & Complex Systems (in classical & quantum contexts)
concepts.description: Study of non-linear dynamical systems, sensitivity to initial conditions, chaos; classical examples (pendulum with driving force, double pendulum, logistic map), and introduction to quantum chaos / semi-classical methods for complex quantum systems.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow users to simulate classical non-linear systems (e.g. driven pendulum, logistic map), observe phase-space, bifurcations, chaos onset; optionally explore simplified quantum-chaos analogues (e.g. quantum kicked rotor).

cross_domain_links.notes: Simulating complex systems computationally benefits from efficient code and system understanding

learning_objectives: Understand behavior of non-linear dynamical systems and criteria for chaos.
Visualize phase-space, bifurcations, and chaotic trajectories.
Explore analogies between classical and quantum chaos in simplified models.

concepts.name: Monte Carlo Simulation: Ising Model & Phase Transitions Project
concepts.description: Implement and analyze the 2D (or 3D) Ising model using Monte Carlo (Metropolis or cluster algorithms), explore temperature-driven phase transition, compute observables (magnetization, susceptibility, specific heat), estimate critical temperature, finite-size effects.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to write a Monte Carlo simulation of Ising model, vary temperature, visualize spin configurations, compute statistical observables, plot magnetization/energy/heat-capacity vs temperature; explore finite-size scaling and critical behavior

learning_objectives: Implement Monte Carlo code for the Ising model.
Compute thermodynamic observables across varying temperature.
Identify and analyze the phase transition (order parameter, critical temperature).
Understand finite-size effects and scaling behavior in statistical systems.

concepts.name: Quantum Monte Carlo Simulation: Bose–Einstein Condensation in a Harmonic Trap
concepts.description: Simulate a dilute gas of bosons in a harmonic potential using Monte Carlo methods to explore condensation behavior, compute critical temperature, condensate fraction vs temperature, study finite-size and density dependence — based on ideal or weakly-interacting Bose gas models.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to simulate bosonic particles under harmonic trap, apply Monte Carlo sampling (or quantum-statistical sampling), compute occupancy of ground state vs excited states as temperature decreases, observe onset of condensation, compute critical temperature, condensate fraction curves.

learning_objectives: Model a gas of bosons in a confining potential using quantum/statistical Monte Carlo.
Compute occupancy distributions and identify macroscopic occupation of ground state (condensate).
Determine critical temperature for condensation and study dependence on density / trap parameters.
Relate simulation results to analytical predictions for ideal Bose gas condensation.

concepts.name: Design-and-Simulate: Simple Particle Accelerator (classical approach)
concepts.description: Design a simplified classical model of a linear (or ring) particle accelerator: simulate charged particles under electromagnetic fields, model beam steering/focusing using fields, study beam dynamics, stability, confinement, and motion under field gradients. This is a toy model, not full high-energy physics, but useful for understanding accelerator physics basics.

simulation_metadata.purpose: Allow user to build a classical particle accelerator simulation: define field regions (electric/magnetic), simulate particle trajectories, apply focusing/steering, explore parameters such as field strength, particle energy, beam divergence, detect stability boundaries and resonance behaviors.

learning_objectives: Construct a simplified classical model of a particle accelerator.
Simulate charged particles under electric and magnetic fields and analyze their trajectories.
Understand beam dynamics: focusing, steering, confinement, and stability.
Explore how varying field parameters affect beam quality and stability.
